speaker: interviewer
text: I'm interviewing Joseph Carver in London, England. Joe, can you tell me how conditions changed in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Once the ghetto was sealed off and we were put to work, food was very scarce. And in order to obtain food, you had to have a ration card. And you could only obtain a ration card if you were registered in the house or in the block where you lived and provided you had a job. You had to have a job in order to get, because otherwise, you were considered a parasite and you couldn't get a ration card.

speaker: interviewee
text: So all of us had jobs except my mother and my little brother. So we had to share our food with our brother and my mother. Later on, facilities were given for housewives who couldn't work, but where the family was at work. They were also given rations. And children were given rations. So this was all right so far. At least more food was coming in the house than before. So a kind of a normal pattern was established.

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was rationed, as I said before. We were given one loaf of bread to last for five days. And it was just a round bread, maybe two kilos in weight-- maybe two kilos in weight, maybe less. I can't remember exactly how it was, what was the weight of it. And we would get like a half a pound of flour, half a pound of sugar. In the beginning, they had semolina. Later on, they had barley. After barley, they had something else, some sort of a substitute. Coffee was not available. Tea was available, but we got kind of coffee extracts, which appeared first in a liquid form and then in a powder form. It wasn't proper coffee. It was like a coffee flavor. They called it the extracts. And it was-- I've seen it after the war in-- in the coffee factories, what people sweep from the floor. That, more or less, was given to us. So we had these meager rations. And we were working. Then, in 1941, the chairman of the ghetto, which-- who was called Rumkowski--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember him before the war, when he was the director of a orphanage. I had a friend who was in that orphanage. And he was in our school. So I invited him, for meals, to my home and usually sometimes went with him to his-- back to his orphanage. And I met Rumkowski there. And he said, where have you been? And he said, oh, this is my friend. I went to his house. I had a meal. Oh, well done, he said. Very nice, very nice. And this was before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: So of course, in the war, when we were already in the ghetto, people said, oh, you know Rumkowski? Nobody could get any access to Rumkowski, although, he was driven around in a carriage. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The coach driver, with his whip, would whip people not to come near the chairman. So it was really very difficult to get a petition even to him. It was very difficult.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, we-- we all had to work. And my father got a job in the [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] vegetable depot. He worked in the vegetable depot. And I-- although I had a job, they told me that if I go in a children's colony-- because I was still went to school. If I went to a children's colony, I would be given clothing, and food, and would be working there, just like on a [NON-ENGLISH], like they have in Israel, where the pioneers get training work for farming, and for agriculture, and for gardening.

speaker: interviewee
text: But prior to that, still being in the ghetto, one of my cousins said, look, let's make soap. Everybody needs soap to wash. All we really need is fat. And fat we can obtain. And we can get bacon. And bacon is obtainable outside the ghetto. How do we get it? So it meant smuggling. As I said, the ghetto was hermetically sealed off. But for smugglers, there is really no border. So this cousin said, I give you a pair of pliers. You can cut with-- the wire with the pliers on your way out and try to come back the same way. However, if you can't come back the same way, you've got the pliers. Come through a different way. Now, my father was very reluctant that I should be going. But I was fearless. I knew we had to eat. And we needed the money. And he had a very good idea making soap. So I took the risk. I went out, I think, about twice or three times. And it became very difficult-- difficult to buy things, difficult to come back into the ghetto, and the third thing-- to be recognized, reason being that once you sort of left the ghetto and you went into the town, in Lodz itself, dressed up as a German-- ethnic German, so to speak of-- you were on your own because your family is in the ghetto. And I was still a child. How old was I then? I was 12-- about 12 years old. But I had the wisdom of Solomon. [LAUGHS] And I knew it was really very difficult for the simple reason that once I was outside, in the middle of the town, it was not too bad. I could buy. I could go in, in the butcher shop, and buy two kilos of bacon, or one kilo here, and one kilo in another shop, and carry it with me on my shoulder. But getting back to the ghetto, where the Poles, on both sides of the road, sized up people going backwards and forwards, seeing their movements, looking at them. Do they look Jewish? Do-- don't they-- don't they look Jewish? Is there something-- are they hiding something, you know? That became very difficult. So after that, I ceased. I didn't go out anymore. I said-- on the whole, I think, I had only done three trips outside. But you made the soap?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we made the soap. I even sold the soap myself. Now my cousin, who later on went to America after the war, he was very good in making the soap. He had the molds. He had the know how and, of course, the fat-- which I supplied. He had the essences. And the soap, when it was fresh, had a very nice smell. We called it Lux Soap. It was green-- sometimes green, sometimes pink, and sometimes white and beige. So there were four colors. But when it was out in the sun too long, the soda would come out. And of course, we had to choose another venue, to go into a different street, in order not to be recognized. Otherwise, the people wanted to have their money back. So this went on for a little while, until the stock lasted. Then, once it was finished, that was the end of the story.

speaker: interviewee
text: So everybody was at work. And we changed work, alternative work. We went from one place to the other. We had to register, of course, in our workplace. And they always asked, why did you leave this? And we had to give a reason. Either we wanted to work with our friends who were also working in the same factory, or we were living in a different vicinity, or we would say we had some domestic problems. We always had to give a reason.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they took us on because-- if they needed workers. So they were only too glad if somebody wanted to come and work in their factory, rather than being in the factory where one was before. So this was no problem. So after a while, I went to that children colony. Because Rumkowski, the chairman-- he was a benefactor to children, especially to orphans. He loved orphans because he never had children of his own. And he tried to protect the children. And he gave them more than the adults.

speaker: interviewee
text: So on that colony, I was well looked after. Now before I came on this colony, I had to go through a medical examination. And this was in the winter. And it was very cold. I didn't have much to wear at all. So the young woman who was the director of the children's colony saw me shivering. She says, oh, better take him inside and give him some clothing. So I was given underwear. I was given a suit, socks and shoes, and an overcoat, and a hat, which was superb, by that standard. But this was only given to the children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to school. Yes, we went to school. And in our spare time, we were trained-- trained in gardening, in agriculture. We had lectures given by tutors. And in the meantime, my family was in the ghetto itself. The children's colony was also in the ghetto, but on the edge, on the edge of the ghetto. So one day, my mother came running with my little brother, because the bigger brother was working. And she told me that my father has passed away. Now, he had typhoid. Also, he died of hunger. Because we had very meager rations in those days. And people tended to eat everything in one go because they were afraid that the goods, the food, would be stolen from them. So they said, well, if we are dying, we might as well have a good meal, and we die in any case. So the death certificate-- the Germans did not allow to put typhoid or starvation on the de-- on the death certificate. Everybody died from natural causes. That was the norm. Even now, so many years after the war, when the Poles issue the death certificate, no mention was made cause of death-- absolutely not.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father died. And my little brother was staying at home. And I and my older brother-- we went after the hearse. Now when we came to the cemetery and they were digging in a grave, there was my aunt, my uncle, and one of my cousins, and just us two boys. But there were many people on the cemetery. And the Jewish law requires that if somebody says kaddish, provided there are 10 people-- 10 male within the vicinity of hearing the name of [? amen, ?] you may recite kaddish. Otherwise, kaddish is forbidden to be said. So my uncle said, your father is dead. Say kaddish. The words wouldn't come out of my mouth. And he shouted at me. He says, honor your father, he says. The tears were rolling down. And I just couldn't say it. I don't know even if my older brother could say it. I can't remember. All I remember is that the tears were rolling down on my face. And no words would come out. And he was really disgusted with me. So after he died-- that was in May. He died in May. And there was me left. I didn't go back to that colony. I stayed at home. Yes, I stayed at home. No, I had to go back. Yes, I had to go back. And while I went back, I got-- what do you call this? Scarlet fever.

speaker: interviewee
text: Scarlet fever was raging at the time. And it was in our block, in the girls' section. And somehow, I got it as well. So I was taken in a isolation hospital, also at the edge of the ghetto-- inside the ghetto, but at the edge. When I mean edge, this means that the barbed wire-- out of our window, from the hospital, if we opened our window, we could see out, which was already, as we said, foreign territory. Poles lived there. The Germans lived in town. But the Poles were living there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was in that hospital for about 10 days. And there were two girls on my ward. And I was the only boy. And one girl-- she got a visit from her mother. And the mother kept telling her that on the children's colony, they took a few hundred children. They took them away by force. They cried. They wouldn't take. German had to come. The SS had to come. They were beating them. And they were deported. Then, she says, in town, the same. And I got scared. Didn't know what to do, how to get out. So I looked at my body and I could see still, I had marks. Because when you got scarlet fever, you get these marks. It's just like having measles.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Jewish doctor disappeared. The nurse also disappeared. Now, it was the job of the nurse to cream us with the special antiseptic cream, all over our body. So when the mother went home, I said to the older girl-- she was about two years older than me. I said to her, listen, there's no doctor. There is no nurse here. And we got these spots. And God knows what is going on. Your mother tells me so many things that are frightening. You can put the ointment on me and I will do it. Oh, she says, I wouldn't hear of this. Modesty, you see. So I said, all right, she will do it. Because there was another girl. So she said, all right. So they done it for me. They covered me with the antiseptic ointment. And I slept. Now, on the second day, I looked. And it was more or less clear. And this mother came again. And she cried that this family was taken away, and this one was jumping, and little children were thrown out from the main hospital, which was not far away from our hospital. So I got terribly scared. And I was looking for my clothing. I couldn't find my clothing. So I found some old, torn trousers-- far too big for me. I put these trousers on. I found a white night shirt. I put that night shirt on. I couldn't find any shoes, so I made myself like a pair of socks, in order to walk. And I ran out of the hospital. I didn't even say to the girls that I was running out. I ran out of the hospital and I went home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have looked like a clown. When I came home-- I didn't go back to the children colony because I heard that they were taking children. So I went-- when I came home, my mother says, oh, I'm so glad to see you. Where were you? And I said, well, I was in that hospital, in that isolation hospital. But thank God, you see, it was already clear. So obviously, I must have been cured of the scarlet fever, because there were no spots, no marks at all. And I put on my usual trousers I had, and my shirt, and my shoes. And my mother said that every day, they are selecting people, at random. They have come to our street. They have taken people at random. But they haven't been into our house. Now, food was very scarce. We couldn't even get our own rations-- first of all, because we didn't have any money. That was number one. Number two, the queues were too big, even to stand. So how do you get food? So every now and again, a horse and one of these wagons would pass, would come from outside the ghetto and go through the ghetto, or a tram car with these [INAUDIBLE] would go. And somebody would sit on the top and, with his feet, kick, so potatoes would fall. And if you were near, you got a potato. But you had to be lucky. Just like in the jungle-- so many animals. Everybody wants to catch the same thing. So we were lucky if we could catch a potato, or a turnip, or a carrot, or a kohlrabi. So if you had one or two of these things, you could go home and cook a little bit of soup, so everybody ate. So this went on for a little while. And then we were looking, what could we sell? There wasn't really very much we could sell. And somehow or the other, by getting the job and going to work, we got paid in coupons. And on these coupons, we could go and get our weekly rations so we had something to eat. Then, going into the Autumn of '42-- we are now in 1942-- we [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] were told not to report to work, that a general action was taking place. Everybody had to be in his abode, where he was living, in the block, and nobody was allowed out. And the director of the block, who was responsible for all the tenants, had to have a list ready that when the Germans came, and they asked for the tenants of room number 1 to number 38, they got to be made available and to come down. So the Germans did catch up. They came into our place. And we had our identity cards from Germany, which was just like a passport. It had the German eagle with the swastika on it. And they had our name. But it looked very official. So when the Germans saw that, they just waved with their hands and that was the end story. Because they would read out your name, and you had your identification, and they thought-- because not many people had it. So they knew that these, most likely, were German Jews, because German Jews had already arrived in '41. When we finished our schooling, we were told that our classes are going to be taken over for quarters of the Jews who will be coming from Germany, from Czechoslovakia, and from Austria and Luxembourg. So it was the end of the school and we were already all working. So that was in '42. Now, my mother hid my little brother under the bed. And he wasn't found. Then, when the Germans left our courtyard, and everything was all right, and we had to go back to our quarters, one of the Jewish militiamen came back, looked under the bed, and found my little brother, and forced to take him out. And my mother was fighting with him. And she wouldn't let go of my little brother's hand. So he took her as well. And we looked out of our window and she was already sitting on the lorry. And she waved to us. And she says, go back, go back. And she had my little brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were being deported. We didn't know, at the time, where you-- where they were going. It's only when we went to Auschwitz we were told by others that all these transports who went from Lodz, at that time, were sent to Chelmno, which was not far-- about 40 kilometers away from Lodz-- and there they were gassed, in these gas vans. Because they didn't have the proper gas ovens and the crematoria. And they were burned in pits. That what we were told from eye witnesses.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I lost my mother and my brother-- my little brother. And I was just there with my older brother. My older brother got a job as a night watchman in the same factory where I was working. And the director of the factory was a friend of my uncle's. He knew the whole family. He also knew my father, who was already dead by then. So he said that everybody got to work. And my brother, who wasn't well-- he said, I'll give you a job as a night watchman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now night watchmen were wearing a special uniform. They had a special cap with the insignia of the night watchmen squad. And they were supposed to look after the factories, so that nobody steals any raw material or sabotages this. And they had to look out, so that there would be no fire, because fire was a hazard. And my brother was there, but he caught pneumonia. He caught typhoid. And he was very, very ill. So he died on the first of November, 1942. At the time, the chairman gave an order to all the directors of the ghetto factories to take in orphans. To alleviate the sufferings in the ghetto, everybody was obliged to take in somebody. And my uncle knew the director of this particular factory. And he spoke to him. And he says, yes, he would take me in. So I came to live with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did the factory make?

speaker: interviewee
text: The factory was making repairs of sewing machines. Because I worked in different places, different factories, but that factory, they were repairing the sewing machines in the ghetto. Because they had large factories where they were making uniforms for the military.
